## reference · promo poster visual hierarchy focal point eye flow scanning
* A clear visual hierarchy guides the eye to the most important elements on the page.
* Visual hierarchy can be created through variations in color and contrast, scale, and grouping.
* Use color saturation to create hierarchy: bright colors for important items, less-saturated colors for less important items.
* Limit color use to 2 primary and 2 secondary colors.
* Use no more than 3 contrast variations for complex designs.
* Do not rely only on color to communicate visual hierarchy, as people with color blindness may not perceive differences.
* Use scale to create visual hierarchy: bigger elements stand out more and attract users' attention.
* Use no more than 3 sizes: small, medium, and large.
* Make the most important element the biggest.
* Use grouping to create visual hierarchy: proximity and common regions help direct attention to relevant areas.
* Let elements breathe: an element with more space around it will be perceived as one group and receive more attention.
* Consider using a container to create visual hierarchy, but use sparingly to avoid clutter.
* Use the squint test to evaluate the conveyed groupings and hierarchy in a design.
* Define the hierarchy of content and key points before designing.
* Apply variations in color and contrast, scale, or grouping to create visual hierarchy.
* Test the design with target users to ensure it reads as intended.
* Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/visual-hierarchy-ux-definition/
